title: Lecture 1: The Nature and Identity of the Church
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: Runtabers Lecture Series on Historic Pre-Millennialism
source_files: Lecture 1_ The Nature and Identity of the Church.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Begin a Series on Pre-Millennialism with the Church?
subpoints: The accusation that historic pre-millennialism is a form of dispensational pre-millennialism is addressed from the outset
The core difference between historic pre-millennialism and dispensationalism is the nature and identity of the church
Historic pre-millennialism is rooted in covenant theology, tying the church to the Abrahamic covenant
Dispensationalism (Ryrie's summary): God pursues two distinct purposes — one for Judaism (earthly people, earthly objectives) and one for Christianity (heavenly people, heavenly objectives)

The lecture series overview: Dr. John Battle on the post-tribulational coming of Christ; Professor Chris Lynch on Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the New Covenant; Pastor Steve Brindiger on the two resurrections

outline.title: The Six Uses of the Word 'Church' in the New Testament
subpoints: The invisible/triumphant church: all true believers through the ages (Hebrews 12:22-23)
The visible/historical/militant church: professing Christians and their children at a point in time (1 Corinthians 1:2)
The local/particular church: believers gathered in one place (Galatians 4:15)
The regional church: a collection of congregations in a geographical area (Acts 9:31)
The organic church: the church emphasizing the people who compose it (1 Corinthians 12-14)
The institutional church: the body entrusted with sacraments, discipline, and worship as 'the pillar and ground of truth' (1 Timothy 3:15)

outline.title: The Church in the Old Testament: A Linguistic and Theological Case
subpoints: The English word 'church' translates only the Greek word ecclesia, leaving its Hebrew equivalents as 'congregation' — a stylistic, not theological, choice by early English translators
The Septuagint (the Greek OT used by first-century Christians) uses ecclesia nearly 300 times to describe Israel as a religious institution
Psalm 22:22 uses ecclesia/congregation — quoted in Hebrews 2:12 as 'the church'
Acts 7:38 (Stephen): Israel in the wilderness is explicitly called 'the church in the wilderness'
Therefore, the argument that the church did not exist in the OT is a linguistic confusion, not a biblical conclusion

outline.title: The Abrahamic Covenant as the Origin of the Church
subpoints: The Belgic Confession's three marks of the true church: faithful preaching of the Word, proper administration of the sacraments, and practice of church discipline

All three marks are present in the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 17): circumcision as the sacrament of the covenant (Romans 4), cutting off those who refuse it as church discipline, and Abraham's command to proclaim God's word to his household (Genesis 18:19)
God established the visible church when he entered into covenant with Abraham — before this, the people of God were not organized into a regulated body of worship
Every subsequent covenant flows from the Abrahamic: Mosaic, Davidic, and New Covenant all expand on but do not replace it

outline.title: The Church as Israel: New Testament Confirmation
subpoints: Galatians 3:5-9: The gospel was preached beforehand to Abraham; those who have faith are the sons of Abraham and are blessed along with Abraham
Galatians 3:15-18: The Abrahamic covenant, confirmed in Christ, cannot be annulled by the Mosaic law 430 years later — the promise that saves us is the Abrahamic promise
Galatians 3:28-29: In Christ there is no Jew or Greek; if you belong to Christ, you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to the promise
Ephesians 2:11-13, 19-22: Gentiles were formerly 'excluded from the commonwealth of Israel'; through Christ's blood they are now 'fellow citizens with the saints'
Romans 11:16-23: Gentile believers are grafted into the same olive tree rooted in Abraham — not a different tree
Hebrews 6:13-18: The oath God swore to Abraham is what gives believers strong encouragement; those who deny Gentile inclusion in the Abrahamic covenant are making God a liar

outline.title: The New Covenant Made with Israel and the Future of Ethnic Israel
subpoints: Jeremiah 31:31-32: The new covenant is explicitly made with 'the house of Israel and the house of Judah'
The book of Hebrews (the longest continuous OT quotation in the NT, Jer. 31 in Heb. 8) proves the new covenant of Christ is the same as that of Jeremiah 31
To be outside Israel is to be outside the new covenant — therefore Gentile believers must be part of Israel
The land promises to Abraham were never fulfilled in his lifetime (Hebrews 11:8-13); the new covenant expands them from Palestine to the whole world (Matthew 5:5)
Historic pre-millennialism allows for — though does not require — a future revival among ethnic Israel, to occur through faith in Jesus Christ
God has one bride, not two; the name of that bride is Israel, the church of the living God

summary:
The first lecture of the Runtabers Lecture Series on Historic Pre-Millennialism addresses what is arguably the most fundamental doctrinal question that distinguishes historic pre-millennialism from dispensational pre-millennialism: the nature and identity of the church. Pastor Tito Lyro argues that the charge often leveled against historic pre-millennialists — that they are merely dispensationalists — rests on a failure to examine the two systems carefully. The decisive difference is that historic pre-millennialism, unlike dispensationalism, is grounded in a covenantal reading of Scripture that insists on the essential unity of Israel and the church.

The lecture begins with a careful survey of the six ways the word 'church' (Greek: ecclesia) is used in the New Testament: the invisible triumphant church, the visible historical church, the local congregation, the regional body of churches, the organic church emphasizing its people, and the institutional church entrusted with word, sacrament, and discipline. This last usage, the institutional church, is the focus of the lecture. Pastor Lyro then demonstrates that because early English translators chose to render ecclesia as 'church' only in the New Testament — leaving its Hebrew equivalents as 'congregation' — the absence of the word 'church' in English Old Testament translations is a translation artifact, not a theological fact. The Septuagint uses ecclesia nearly 300 times for Israel; Acts 7:38 explicitly calls Israel in the wilderness 'the church.'

The lecture's doctrinal center is the thesis that God established the visible church at the Abrahamic covenant. Using the Belgic Confession's three marks of the true church (preaching, sacrament, and discipline), Pastor Lyro demonstrates that all three are present in Genesis 17-18: circumcision as the sacrament of the covenant, the cutting off of the uncircumcised as discipline, and Abraham's command to proclaim God's word to his household. From Abraham forward, every subsequent covenant — Mosaic, Davidic, New — flows from and expands the Abrahamic without annulling it.

The New Testament evidence confirms this unity. Galatians 3 announces that the gospel was preached to Abraham, that those who believe are his sons, and that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek. Ephesians 2 declares that Gentiles, formerly excluded from the commonwealth of Israel, have been made fellow citizens through Christ's blood. Romans 11 pictures Gentile believers grafted into the same olive tree. The lecture closes by showing that the new covenant was made with the house of Israel (Jeremiah 31) and that the book of Hebrews — through its extended quotation of Jeremiah 31 — proves this is the very covenant inaugurated by Christ. Therefore, to be part of the new covenant is to be part of Israel: there is one people of God, one bride of Christ, whose name is Israel, the church of the living God.
transcript:
## Welcome and Introduction to the Lecture Series

Welcome to our first lecture of this year's Runtabers Lecture Series. We are glad that you join us this morning or whenever you watch this lecture, and we're very excited that you are invested in our seminary enough to listen to these lectures. Our topic for this series is historic pre-millennialism: the unity of the Scriptures brought together in this particular eschatological position.

So as we begin this series, we begin with a question: Why start a series of lectures on historic pre-millennialism with a lecture on the church? Should we look at the book of Revelation or one of the apocalyptic prophets in order to talk about eschatology? Well, we could do that and we will do that in future lectures. Dr. John Battle is going to look at the coming of Christ and how it is a post-tribulational coming of Christ. Professor Chris Lynch is going to look at Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Revelation in the New Covenant. And Pastor Steve Brindiger is going to look at the fact that the Scriptures teach that there are going to be two resurrections in the future — the resurrection of the righteous and the resurrection of the unrighteous.

It is important, however, that a series of lectures on historic pre-millennialism begins with a consideration of the nature and identity of the church, because that is the area in which unfounded accusations are made against this particular eschatological position. The accusation levied against historic pre-millennialism is that we are dispensational pre-millennialists. The accusation is often brought by the less informed — those that have not taken the time to actually look at the differences between those two positions.

The truth is that there is a world of difference between these two systems, and at the core of that difference is the nature and identity of the church. Historic pre-millennialism is born out of a covenantal understanding of the Scriptures that ties the church with the Abrahamic covenant. And this is anything but dispensationalism.

Ryrie, who is considered by many the father of modern dispensationalism, says the following in his book (pages 44-46): "What then is the sine qua non of dispensationalism? The answer is threefold. A dispensationalist keeps Israel and the church distinct." He summarizes: "The dispensationalists believe that throughout the ages God is pursuing two distinct purposes: one related to the earth with earthly people and earthly objectives involved, which is Judaism; the other is related to heaven with heavenly people and heavenly objectives involved, which is Christianity."

He continues: "This is probably the most basic theological test of whether or not a man is a dispensationalist. A man who fails to distinguish Israel and the church will inevitably not hold to dispensational distinctions."

Although we might argue with Ryrie about the uniqueness of some of his points to dispensationalism, we say a hearty amen to his assessment that the main difference between dispensationalism and other millennial positions is its view of the church in relation to Israel. Historic pre-millennialism rejects this absolute distinction between Israel and the church. And that's what I'm going to try to set forth to you in this lecture — the identity of the church and the nature of the church as it relates to the whole of the Scriptures.

## The Six Uses of 'Church' in the New Testament

I would like to start by doing a word study on the word "church." How is the word "church" used in the Bible? Now, as we do that, it's important that we realize that the word "church" is not used in the Bible in a unified sense — it doesn't always mean the same thing. I think that particular word "church" is used in at least six different ways in the Bible.

It is used to describe the collection of all true believers through the ages — what we might call the invisible church, or the triumphant church. A text that uses the word church in that way is Hebrews 12:22-23, where the author says: "you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to innumerable angels in festal gathering, and to the church of the firstborn, who are enrolled in heaven, and to God, the judge of all, and to the spirits of the righteous made perfect."

Another way the word "church" is used is to refer to the collection of professing Christians and their children at a point in time — what we call the visible church, or the historical or militant church. A common passage: 1 Corinthians 1:2, "give no offense to Jews or to Greeks or to the church of God."

A third way the Bible uses the word "church" is to refer to the gathering of believers in the same place — the local or particular church. Colossians 4:15: "give my greetings to the brothers in Laodicea, and to Nympha and the church in her house."

Fourthly, the word "church" refers to a collection of several churches in a geographical area. In Acts 9:31: "So the church throughout all Judea and Galilee and Samaria had peace and was being built up."

The word "church" is also used to refer to the church as an organism, emphasizing the people in it — the organic church. That's how Paul uses it in 1 Corinthians 12 through 14.

And the word "church" is used to talk about the church as an institution — to which the sacraments were given, which exercises discipline, to which corporate worship was given, which is called the ground and pillar of truth. That's how Paul refers to the church in 1 Timothy 3:15: "I write so that you may know how you ought to conduct yourself in the house of God, which is the church of the living God, the pillar and ground of the truth."

This lecture develops this last way of thinking about the church. What is it? When did it begin? What is its nature? Who is part of it?

## The Church in the Old Testament

Now some may ask: since the word "church" is not easily found in the Old Testament, should we assume that it didn't exist in the Old Testament? Though this is a common question, it is not based on a full understanding of how the biblical languages work.

For reasons beyond the scope of this lecture, the earliest English translators chose to use the English word "church" to translate only the Greek word ecclesia, leaving its Hebrew equivalents to be translated by other English words such as "congregation." This was a stylistic, not a theological or linguistic, decision.

The Septuagint — the very early Greek translation of the Old Testament used by first-century Christians and New Testament writers — uses the word ecclesia almost 300 times to describe Israel as a religious institution. One example: Psalm 22:22 — "I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you." The Septuagint uses ecclesia for "congregation." If we were being consistent with New Testament translation, this verse would read: "in the midst of the church I will praise you."

The Holy Spirit inspired the author of Hebrews, who uses the Septuagint rendering. Hebrews 2:12 quotes Psalm 22:22 and says: "I will declare your name to my brethren; in the midst of the church I will sing praise to you."

Stephen further confirms this in Acts 7:38, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit: "This is he who was in the church in the wilderness with the Angel who spoke to him on Mount Sinai, and with our fathers, who received the living oracles to give to us." The Old Testament Israel in the wilderness is explicitly called "the church."

Therefore we can put to rest the argument that the church did not exist in the Old Testament. The argument simply does not make linguistic sense.

## The Abrahamic Covenant and the Origin of the Church

Virtually all Presbyterian and Reformed bodies accept the Belgic Confession's definition of the marks of a true church. The marks by which the true church is known are these: if the pure doctrine of the gospel is preached therein; if she maintains the pure administration of the sacraments as instituted by Christ; if church discipline is exercised in punishing sin.

Using this broadly accepted definition, we can identify the first time all three elements are present in one institution. And the first time we find all three elements present in the Bible is in the covenant God makes with Abraham, particularly in Genesis chapter 17.

In Genesis 17:9-13, Abraham is commanded to administer the sacrament of circumcision to all who are eligible — every male in his household. Romans 4 says that circumcision was the "sign and seal of the covenant" — sacramental language from the Westminster Standards. So we have the proper administration of the sacrament.

Genesis 17:14: "Any uncircumcised male who is not circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin shall be cut off from his people. He has broken my covenant." He who refuses the sacrament is to be cut off — that is church discipline.

Genesis 18:19: "For I have chosen him, that he may command his children and his household after him to keep the way of the LORD by doing righteousness and justice." That is the preaching, the proclamation, of the word of God to his household.

All three marks of the true church are present in the Abrahamic covenant. That is the beginning of the visible church. Before Abraham, people were calling on the name of the Lord, but the people of God were not organized into a regulated body of worship until God enters into covenant with Abraham. And from that point forward, every subsequent covenant — Mosaic, Davidic, New — flows from the Abrahamic covenant.

## The Church as Israel: New Testament Evidence

Notice how the New Testament speaks of our salvation as the result of the Abrahamic covenant. Paul goes out of his way to say that those justified by faith in Jesus have Abraham for their father. Romans 4 — the faith through which we are justified is the same faith Abraham exercised. The promise that saves us is the Abrahamic promise.

Galatians 3:5-9: "Just as Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness — know then that it is those of faith who are the sons of Abraham. And the Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham, saying, 'In you shall all the nations be blessed.' So then, those who are of faith are blessed along with Abraham, the man of faith."

Galatians 3:15-18: The Abrahamic covenant, confirmed in Christ, cannot be annulled by the Mosaic law given 430 years later. The promise through which we are saved is the Abrahamic promise.

Galatians 3:28-29: "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus. And if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise."

People of God — those of you in the church of Jesus Christ today — you are heirs of Abraham, members of Abraham's household, descendants of Abraham by faith, according to the covenant that God made with him. Our hope today depends on the Abrahamic covenant being an everlasting covenant that applies to Gentiles as much as to Jews.

Ephesians 2:11-13: "Remember that at one time you Gentiles in the flesh... were at that time separated from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world. But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ."

Then verses 19-22: "So then you are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God." Paul says explicitly in verse 19 that one who believes in Christ becomes a citizen of Israel.

Romans 11: Gentile believers have been grafted into the same olive tree that started with Abraham. There is not an Israel tree and a church tree — the church is the same tree, a continuation of Israel. We are grafted into that tree, not some other tree.

## The New Covenant Made with Israel

There is something we sometimes miss: the new covenant was made with Israel. If we're not part of Israel, we're not part of the new covenant.

Jeremiah 31:31-32: "Behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah, not like the covenant that I made with their fathers on the day when I took them by the hand to bring them out of the land of Egypt."

Our Lord Jesus Christ says that his supper is the sign and seal of this new covenant: "This cup is the new covenant in my blood" (1 Corinthians 11:25). Some try to argue that the new covenant Paul speaks of is different from Jeremiah 31 — but the whole book of Hebrews refutes this. The longest continuous Old Testament quotation in the New Testament is Jeremiah 31:31-34 in Hebrews chapter 8, where the inspired author says that Jesus Christ inaugurated that new covenant by his blood for all who believe. Hebrews 9:15: "He is the mediator of a new covenant."

Hebrews 11:8-13 says that Abraham died without receiving the land promises: "These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth." The new covenant, a better covenant, expands the land promise from Palestine to the whole world — that's why Christ says in the Beatitudes, "Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth" (Matthew 5:5). These land promises will be fulfilled at the coming of Christ during his millennial kingdom, and we — Gentile believers grafted into Israel — will be part of the fulfillment.

## Conclusion: One Bride, One People

As we come to a close: it is important that we realize the promises made to Abraham are for the church. He is our God and we are his people. God has always been after his people. And those who have always been his people — from the time of Adam — are those circumcised in the heart.

Where God will do a work of revival among ethnic Israel is something this view of the church allows for but does not require. We can differ on that. But this view does allow for a future revival with ethnic Israel, and if God does work a special work of revival among ethnic Israel, it will be through Jesus Christ, as it has always been.

People of God: our God has one bride and one bride only. He is not a bigamist with an earthly bride and a heavenly bride. The name of the bride is Israel — the church of the living God.
